[Note: The image of this page is upside down. The following transcription is of the text oriented correctly. The page appears to be a heavily overwritten draft of a letter, and much of it is illegible.]

It is with much pleasure [unclear] a grateful [unclear] to you [unclear] my sincere thanks for the very high honour you have done me [unclear] Although it was a great gratification to me [unclear] yet I shall not forget to take [unclear] many friendly tokens of your esteem. [unclear] my name published [unclear] so highly beneficial to research [unclear] & so encouraging to [unclear] that this work the 1st in the whole world has been so complete for [unclear] although [unclear] also for many tokens of [unclear] enjoy my esteem. [unclear]

[At top right of letter:]
To Great Britain

Honorable Gentlemen

I beg leave to forward my grateful thanks to your Honourable Board for your award determined to me on the 5^th inst. I cannot fully express myself yet you may be assured that the thanks of the Lords Commissioners of the admiralty, added to your honourable conformation of my having been at all useful in so great a concern to my nation as the British Navy - it binds me with the most ardent desire to do my duty and I shall be happy at all times to meet the approbation of a Tribunal of so much experience as your Honourable Board. May you enjoy a continuance of happy years and the British Navy flourish under your arduous endeavours.

I am
Hon: Gent^n
Your very faithful
humble Servant
Ja^s Sowerby

[Circular mark at top center: Copy]

11^th Oct. 1814

Honourable Gentlemen

I have the pleasure of sending the sixteen Hygrometers coloured. It would be wrong not to say a word or two about them and to observe that in all probability they would spoil where wood is liable to mischief. I therefore recommend that when not in immediate use they should be preserved in a moderate temperature and in such a situation where the red indicates a safe medium: And I would intimate that it is rather safer to depend upon yellow as [inserted above: too] dry, and blue as generally too damp for the preservation of wood above water as rather more accurate than the whole scale of the instrument, allowing from the red on either side the degrees of dry & wet as more dangerous as the hand is [inserted above: distant] in degrees from it. Thus if getting too dry, assist it with shade &c & if too wet with a proportionate medium of air.

I am Honourable Gentlemen, Your very obed^t Serv^t
Ja^s Sowerby

[Address panel]

[Top, very faint:] [unclear: On Public Service?]

[Large numeral:] 2

James Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place
Lambeth. –

[Bottom left:] Navy Office

[Note: Red circular postmark on left, illegible.]

Navy Office 23^d March 1814. –

Sir,

Commissioner Cunningham having
transmitted to us a Letter from the Master
Shipwright of Deptford Yard, stating that you
have expressed a wish that the space
chosen by you for Stowing Timber conformably
to your view, should be paved with Purbeck
Stone, and represented, [inserted above: that] in conversation
with you, he proposed, [inserted above: that] the space in question
should be paved with common Stone, to which
you objected; – We request you will let us know
what are your Objections to the Ground being
paved with common Stone strewed over with
Smiths Cinders. –

We are,
Sir,
Your humble Servants
T B Thompson
F. T. Hartwell
H Legge

James Sowerby Esq^r

[Address panel]

[Top, very faint:] [unclear: On Public Service?]

James Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place,
Lambeth. –

[Bottom left:] Navy Office.

[Note: Red circular postmark on left, illegible. Black ink blot on left. Stain on right.]

[at top right:] Navy Office 5^th. January 1814.

Sir,

Having had under consideration your letter of the
Ultimo, We acquaint you that we have awarded to
you the sum of Two hundred Guineas for the
services which you have rendered in order to
prevent as much as possible the alarming
increase of the Dry Rot in His Majesty's
Ships, and to introduce an improved
mode of seasoning and stowing away
Timber: At the same time we deem
it necessary to apprize you that in
awarding this sum we do not attach
to you the merit of having saved
the Queen Charlotte by your advice,
all the defective Materials having
[at bottom right:] been

[at bottom left:] James Sowerby Esq^r.

been taken out of that Ship at
Plymouth, where she was repaired
there. -

We are,
Sir,
Your humble Servants
[signed:] T.B.Thompson
[signed:] R. Seppings
[signed:] H Legge


[in a different hand, at bottom:]
Rec^d the money at the Bank Jan 15. 1813 -
J.S.
[Blank page with fold marks and a faint, embossed watermark in the center.]

1817

Sept 15 went to the Navy office at Mr Lukins request who took me up to private stairs to one of his head workmen to ask my opinion privately upon regarding some of his difficulties and to instruct him in my opinion regarding forcing in certain projections. I call'd with Captain Tomlinson in a chaise told Mr Lukin's people under all circumstances I should give my advice or opinion. Told me to send my acct to him, which I had twice call'd to fruitless.

Honourable Gentlemen

Presuming on the evident improvement in the Dockyards (in many respects) and in their agreement with my [inserted above: suggestions] reports and on the judgement of the high abilities that have referred to them after so long attentively considering them; Not forgetting the thanks of the Lords of the Admiralty so handsomely expressed for [text crossed out: by which she was saved] my report of the 4^th August regarding the Queen Charlotte) and the promising hopes of a thorough and comfortable prevention of [text crossed out: this] the dry rot and a great saving in future, I trouble your Honourable Board with a short memorial laying before you a [text crossed out: short] statement of part of two years attention [unclear: paid?] paid to a subject of such national consequence that I cannot for even a moment forget it, and the urgency of which has frequently interrupted [inserted above: some of] my periodical works to the disappointment of my friends and consequently my own loss. Confident of your consideration according to your wonted wisdom and that I shall be so encouraged for my zeal as that I may persevere in your candid desires with the best effects I am

Honourable Gentlemen your very obed^t Servant

Jas Sowerby

* It was generally supposed that the Dryrot had rendered this ship incurable, and she is now on service, [inserted above: providentially] hence saved by my directions; I need not mention the value of such saving to those conversant with the subject; it would certainly be enough to afford ample indemnification for my past services, but also for future ones, [inserted above: and] and reckoning upon other first rates [inserted above: &c] assuredly saved and to be saved by such an example,
properly applied
the amount would be immense.

* On this report I shall be happy to see your desires put in practice and I have every hope that what may now be done posterity will reap the benefit, and [inserted above: upon] your Board their blessing.

A short Statement
&c.

1812
July 16 Attended the Honourable Commissioners of H. M. Navy according to their request transmitted by Mr. Nelson.

17 Recieved directions to proceed to Plymouth with Mr. Lukin, as early as possible, to inspect the H. M. S. the Queen Charlotte which had recieved considerable injury from the Dryrot, and to advise the prevention of its further progress, if possible; * to report thereon and to extend my report to the means of prevention on board H. M. Ships in future.

21 Proceeded to Plymouth in company with Mr. Lukin attended the Dockyard and H. M. S. the Queen Charlotte; visited Portsmouth Dockyard in the way and arrived at Home Aug. 2 being Sunday.

Aug 4 Delivered my report on the Queen Charlotte, pointing out the possibility of repairing and recommending for that purpose [text crossed out: that a construction to a free current of air,] stretching an awning over the whole ship so as to be placed as to keep of rain and to admit a current of air beneath it in order to remove all moisture; objecting

to the use of artificial heat as likely to encrease the evil, but recommending ventilation promoted by stoves so placed as not to heat the Timber [inserted above: under] certain restrictions: recommending the removal of all infected wood, suggesting the necessity of using the [inserted above: best] seasoned Timber in the repairing and keeping this as dry as possible, putting on the boiled planks in such a manner that as little moisture as possible might be confined, that no part should be painted or varnished before it was perfectly dry – Objecting to chemical impregnations as useless and even detrimental.

Aug. 28 Recd the thanks of the Lords of the Admiralty

1813
Jany 28 Answered application from your Hon: Board requesting my opinion of Mr. Parkers letter stating that he has discovered the method of curing the Dryrot

Feby 17 Presented my report on the measures necessary to be taken for the prevention of the evil of Dryrot on board H. M. S. in future, recommending the proper seasoning of Timber, that the Trees be carefully inspected & if found free from Fungi, should be seasoned as soon as

felled, the seasoning to be carried on regularly until the last moment of fitting together without artificial heat, [text crossed out: that they should be sheltered from rain & the heat of the sun and supported from the ground; that they should be kept separate from each other as much as possible until quite dry;] that they should be stacked upon high ground; recommending the free access of air through the stacks, not only to prevent fermentation & the growth of Fungi, but to [text crossed out: haisen the wood,] also that the Ships should be frequently cleaned [text crossed out: and aired] and all suspicious parts examined with the Hygrometer in order to discover the earliest indication of the Dryrot.

March 24 Further information given as to the address of the makers of Hygrometer

Oct: 25 Attended Commissioner Seppings to Deptford & Woolwich Dock yards according to desire of the your Hon: Board expressed on the 16th

29 Presented my report thereon, pointing out how far my suggestions relating to stacking of Timber could be carried into execution &c

To the Commissioners of H. M. Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen

I received your letter of the 24^th Inst.
noticing your desires regarding stacking of Timber in
Deptford Dock yard. I will pay the earliest
possible attention to it and am

Honourable Gentlemen

Your very obedient
humble Servant

25 Nov^r 1813

2 Mead Place
Lambeth
Dear Sir

[Address panel of a folded letter]

[Top:]
On H M Service

[Center:]
Ja^s Sowerby Esq^r
2 Mead Place
Lambeth

[Left side, written vertically:]
Navy Office

[Postmark, stamped on left:]
[Circular stamp: 9 o'Clock / 25.NO / 1813 M]

Navy Office 24^th Nov^r 1813.

Sir,

Having attentively considered the two Reports from you, respecting the mode of stowing Timber in the Dock Yards, We direct you will give the necessary instructions to the Officers of Deptford Dock Yard for the erection of six stacks of Timber, to be housed over according to your plan; and we acquaint you that the Officers of that Yard have had directions to afford you every assistance in their power in the prosecution of this business.–

We are,
Sir,
Your humble Servants,
F.T. Hartwell
R Seppings
H Legge

James Sowerby Esq^r.
2, Mead Place
Lambeth.

[This page is the address panel of the folded letter from page 19.]

[Postmark: Red circular stamp, "8 O'Clock / 1813 / MY"]
[Postmark: Black stamp, partially legible, "[...] PAID [...]"]

[Address written sideways:]
James Sowerby Esq^r.
2, Mead Place
Lambeth.

[Below address, also sideways:]
Navy Office –

Navy Office 16^th October 1813.

Sir,

With reference to your reports on the premature decay of His Majesty's Ships, in which you have made several observations with respect to the mode of stowing Timber; We request that you will attend one of the Surveyors of the Navy to Deptford and Woolwich Yards any Morning in the early part of next week, in order that you may see the mode of stowing Timber in those Dock Yards, and point out how far your suggestions can be carried into execution. –

James Sowerby Esq^r.
2 Mead Place, Lambeth.

[Bottom right corner:]
We

We are,

Sir,

Your humble Servants

F. T. Hartwell
H. Legge
T. B. Thompson

Honourable Gentlemen

According to your desire with reference to my report of the 17^th ult^o. I can only say at present that M^r Jones of Oxendon Street, mathematical instrument maker who was a pupil of Ramsdens, made the instrument I used and at my desire is about to make some to revolve 10 times, equal to 1000 parts (instead of odd numbers) which will save the trouble of calculation and agree with the trials by which I calculated the wetness that is mischievous &c. I waited thus long expecting to see one every day.

De Luc's instrument is to be had of M. Hausmann of Cumberland Lodge, G^t Windsor Park, with improvements and the calculations relating to the Thermometer, which when printed may render his instrument preferable in the opinions of some persons.

I am

Honourable Gentlemen

Your very obed^t humble Serv^t

Ja^s Sowerby

2 Mead Place
Lambeth
1813 March 24^th

Navy Office 12^th March 1813.-

Sir,

With reference to your Report of the 1^st Ult^o
relative to Dry Rot in Timber: We request
you will state to us the names and address
of the makers of the Hygrometers which you
would recommend to be used, corresponding
with the calculations of wetness and different
degrees of moisture, as enumerated in that Report.

We are,
Sir,
Your humble Servants,

F. T. Hartwell
H Peake
H Legge

[Pencil annotation in left margin:]
D^r Lulls
J. Haumann.
Camberwell
Lodge Windsor great Park

[Addressed in bottom left corner:]
James Sowerby Esq^r
2, Mead Place,
Lambeth

To the Hon^ble Com^rs of his Maj^s Navy

Hon^ble Gentlemen
I have rec^d your letter requesting my opinion
on M^r Lulkins stating his having discovered the means of preventing
the Dry rot in Timber you must of course be somewhat aware
of the Delicacy of my situation, and see that to give an
opinion upon his discovery is as difficult as to interpret
Nebuchadnezzars Dream, Conscious of your previous information
and Judgement, I venture to presume that you did not before
understand the Dry rot in ships, is unanimously admitted to
be caused by an Insect This and that follows therefore
as I suppose you are well aware how much those gentlemen
is likely properly to understand of this serious concern
[inserted above: which has seriously tended to expose all]

Than Honbinable Gentlemen
your obt H^ble sersen^t
J S

28 Jan^y 1813

[Address panel of a folded letter]

[Postmark 1: red circular stamp]
FREE
JA 27
1813

[Postmark 2: red circular stamp]
JA 27
1813

[Postmark 3: black circular stamp]
LOMBARD S^T OFFICE
[...]

[Pencil annotation:]
On

[Address:]
James Sowerby Esq^r
Navy Office

[Ink annotation, bottom right:]
121/m.

Navy Office 26^th January 1813.

Sir,

We enclose you the copy of a letter
dated the 7^th instant, from M^r J. M.
Parker, of the Little Carron Colour Works
at Leith, stating that he has discovered
the means for preventing the Dry Rot
in Timber; and request you to favor
us with your opinion thereon. –

We are,
Sir,
Your humble Servants,
F.T. Hartwell
H. Peake
H. Legge

James Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place
Lambeth.

Copy

Gent^n

The dry rot in Ships, having lately become a
great national loss, besides an incalculable
disappointment, it therefore becomes an object of more
than common importance, to hit upon some mode of
preventing the same – Imagination sometimes leads us
to attempt the performance of impossibilities, and an
attempt to eradicate, or prevent this evil, may be
considered futile or purely imaginary, having hitherto
baffled the most ingenious, yet it may not be
impossible, and I trust my present idea, will not
appear to be the result of illguided imagination, and
I feel assured that any thought, likely to attain so
desirable a purpose, will not be discarded by you. –
The dry rot in Ships, is universally admitted to be
caused by an Insect; if so, the remedy will not offer
much difficulty; but on the contrary, should it be a
natural defect of the wood, or occasioned by the
partial exclusion of atmospheric air, or some other
cause we cannot immediately account for, still, it
does not argue the same cannot be prevented. I
never have seen a Ship in this situation, but I
have repeatedly seen Oak Timber, in various
situations

The Hon^ble Commissioners
of the Navy

situations completely mouldered away, except in some
particular spots, and I presume for the same
cause as in Ships: and in these instances, my
attention has been drawn to the part which
appeared in the decayed mass, perfect, and in
such case, it has been the object of my
pursuit, to find out the cause of such partial
preservation, and by which, I flatter myself
I have found out the means, at a comparative
small expense, to radically prevent the cause.

Having had the honor to correspond with you on
a former occasion, I thought it proper to make the
present communication to you; feeling assured you
will give the matter that attention, which its importance
deserves – If it should be found necessary, I could wait
upon you, in order to explain the means I propose,
and one which, I shall be glad of the honor of applying.

I am &c.
J.M. Parker
Little Carron Colour Works
Leith 7^th January 1813.–

The experiments I have made on Wood far
advanced in the dry rot, has been
universally successfull —

If any reference should be necessary, I
beg to mention that I am a relation
of M^r Madgshon, late one of the Masters
of Chatham Yard. —

are in consequence to be used generally, we
request that you will state to us the
names and address's of the makers of those
Instruments whose construction you prefer.

It is intended to print such parts of
your report as apply to the preservation of
Ships, in order that they may be circulated
among the Officers of the several Yards,
but we request you will inform us whether
you have any further observations to offer
on the subject in order that the Publication
may be complete.

We have now the pleasure to mention
that we are directed by the Lords Commission
-ers of the Admiralty to state to you that
they consider you entitled to their thanks

and approbation for your attention and
Report. —

We are

Your affectionate Friends.

F. T. Hartwell
H Legge
T B Thompson

Navy Office 28 August 1812.

M^r Sowerby

Having furnished the Lords Commissioners
of the Admiralty with a Copy of your report
dated the 14^th Instant relative to the measures
necessary to be adopted to prevent the progress
of the dry rot in His Majesty's Ship Queen
Charlotte, their Lordships have been pleased
to direct that the means which you have
recommended shall be carried into execution
in repairing that Ship, as well as in building
and repairing His Majesty's Ships at
the several Yards;

[Faint text in a different hand at bottom of page:]

Meads Place

[unclear: and as by promoters]
[unclear: over their Systems Lambeth]

Plymouth July 25. 1812

To the President and [inserted above: Comissioners of] his majesty's
Navy board

Gentlemen, It is with much concern, I am in duty
bound to state to you that the [text crossed out: dread] progress of the evil
on board his majestys Ship Queen Charlotte
[text crossed out: (which so much injures her, is so deeply planted)]
that it is in all respects an extreme nice point
to know what should be done, and it requires
much serious attention to guard against it in
future [inserted above: meaning the remedy for,] [text crossed out: not] yet considering the labor practicable
upon natural principles would hope rather than
despair

M^r Lukin with the most ardent attention
pointed out to me the present state of the
ship and it is my opinion it may be repaired
allowing for some exceptions which I fear cannot
[inserted above: probably]
in the state of things be avoided.

I write this to show that I have entered on the

[*Address panel of a folded letter.*]

[*Red circular postmark, top left: FREE / AU 27 / 1812*]

[*Red circular postmark, bottom right: C / AU 28 / 1812*]

J. Sowerby

Mr. G. B. Sowerby
Mead Place Lambeth
Surrey.

No. 2.

Plymouth July 25. 1812

To the President and [inserted above: comissioners] [text crossed out: gentlemen] of his majestys Navy board

Gentlemen,
It is with much concern, I am in Duty bound to state to you that the disease of the evil on board his majestys Ship Queen Charlotte [text crossed out: Shews so much inside her,] is so deeply planted that it is in all respects an exceeding nice point to know what should be done, and it requires much serious attention to guard against it in future. yet [inserted above: knowing the remedy for] [text crossed out: something] the latter practicable upon natural principles would help rather than despair

Mr Lukin with the most ardent attention pointed out to me the present state of the Ship and it is my opinion it may be repaired allowing for some exceptions which I fear cannot [inserted above: probably] be avoided.

I will try to shew that I have entered on the

[*Address panel of a folded letter.*]

Public Service

Sowerby Esqr.
Mead Place
Lambeth.

Navy Office.

Navy Office 17^th July 1812.

Sir,

We desire you will proceed with M^r Lionel
Lukin to Plymouth, as early as possible, and
inspect the state of His Majesty's Ship Queen
Charlotte, which has received very considerable
injury by the dry rot, with a view to ascertain
what measures it may be advisable to adopt to
stop its further progress if possible, by preventing
the vegetation and spreading of the Fungi.

We further desire that your Report on this
subject may be extended to the measures
necessary to prevent this evil in any of His
Majesty's Ships: and that you will make it
either in conjunction with M^r Lukin or
separately as you may find most convenient.

We are, Sir,
Your humble Servants
F.T. Hartwell
H. Legge
T.B. Thompson

Sowerby Esq^r
2 Mead Place
Lambeth.

[Address panel of a folded letter]

On H.M. Service

Sowerby Esq^r
2 Mead Place
near the Asylum
Lambeth-

[Written sideways on the left:]
Navy Office

Navy Office 15^th July 1812-

Sir,

I have the commands of the
Commissioners of the Navy to request
you will do them the favor to
attend at this Office tomorrow at
three O'Clock.

I am,

Sir,
Your very humble Servant
J.W. Nelson

J.W.

Sowerby Esq^r
2 Mead Place
Lambeth
near the Asylum
